title: Colored Chairs
description:
There are n chairs arranged in a circle. Each chair is either red (R) or blue (B). Chairs are numbered 1..n and chair n is next to chair 1. Your task is to find two adjacent chairs that have the same color.

Interactive version (original): You can query the color of a chair and finally report an index i such that chairs i and i+1 share the same color. n is odd and at most 2e5, and you can ask at most 20 queries.

For this learning exercise, we will solve the offline equivalent: the full coloring is provided as a string of length n over {R,B}. Output any index i (1 ≤ i ≤ n) such that chair i and chair i+1 (with wrap-around for n) have the same color.

If multiple answers exist, print any one of them.

examples.explanation: Pairs: (1,2)=B,R; (2,3)=R,R -> match at i=2; earliest valid index is 2.

constraints: Colors string consists only of 'R' and 'B'
Output any i such that colors[i] == colors[(i % n) + 1]

mcqChain.questionText: With n=5 and colors RBRBR placed around a circle, which adjacent pair has the same color?
mcqChain.explanation: In a circle, 5 and 1 are adjacent. Both are R here, so (5,1) matches. Others are alternating.

wrongExplanations.0: 1 is R and 2 is B, so they differ.
wrongExplanations.1: 2 is B and 3 is R, so they differ.

mcqChain.hint: Remember the wrap-around: chair 5 is next to chair 1.

mcqChain.questionText: If n is odd, can all adjacent pairs in the circle be different (i.e., strict alternation all the way around)?

options.label: Yes, strict alternation is always possible.
No, strict alternation around an odd cycle forces a same-color neighbor.
Only if there are more reds than blues.
Only if there are more blues than reds.

mcqChain.explanation: Alternation flips colors each step; after an odd number of steps you return to the starting color, making the last and first equal.

wrongExplanations.0: Alternation around a cycle of odd length contradicts the wrap-around.
wrongExplanations.2: Counts don't affect this parity argument.

mcqChain.hint: Think about toggling color n times and comparing positions n and 1.

mcqChain.questionText: For n=4 and colors RBRB in a circle, do any adjacent pairs have the same color?

options.label: No; with even n, strict alternation is possible.

mcqChain.explanation: Even length allows full alternation: all adjacent pairs differ here.

wrongExplanations.0: 4 is B and 1 is R; they differ.
wrongExplanations.1: 2 is B and 3 is R; they differ.

mcqChain.hint: Check each pair including (4,1).

mcqChain.questionText: Given n=6 and colors RRB RBB (indices 1..6), which minimal index i is valid to output (1-based) such that i and i+1 are the same color?
mcqChain.explanation: (1,2) are both R, so i=1 works and is the smallest.

wrongExplanations.1: (2,3) are R and B, not equal.
wrongExplanations.3: (5,6) are B and B which works, but 1 is smaller.

mcqChain.hint: Scan from i=1 upward and stop at the first match.

mcqChain.questionText: Do we need to inspect all chairs to find a valid answer?

options.label: Yes, to guarantee correctness we must check all pairs.
No, we can stop as soon as we find any adjacent equal pair.
Only if the first half has no matches.
Only if the last two are the same.

mcqChain.explanation: We can stop at the first valid index since any valid index is acceptable.

wrongExplanations.0: We don't need all pairs; any one valid pair suffices.

mcqChain.hint: The task asks for any valid i, not the count of all such i.

mcqChain.questionText: Given the full colors string, what straightforward approach will always find a valid i if it exists?

options.label: Check each adjacent pair in order (including wrap-around) and stop at the first match.
Randomly pick indices until a match occurs.
Sort the chairs by color and choose two consecutive in the sorted order.
Build a frequency map of R and B and pick the majority color index.

mcqChain.explanation: A single pass checking neighbors finds a valid pair; sorting or randomness are unnecessary.

wrongExplanations.2: Sorting destroys adjacency information from the circle.
wrongExplanations.3: Counts don't tell where equal neighbors occur.

mcqChain.hint: Think of scanning with i and i+1, noting the circle wrap-around at the end.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time complexity of scanning all adjacent pairs once (including the wrap-around)?
mcqChain.explanation: We examine each index a constant number of times: total work proportional to n.

wrongExplanations.1: No dividing the search range; we visit all indices.

mcqChain.hint: You look at each i once and do O(1) work per step.

mcqChain.questionText: Why does a valid i always exist when n is odd?

options.label: Because there are more chairs to the left than to the right.
Because strict alternation around an odd cycle is impossible, forcing a same-color neighbor.
Because there must be more of one color.

mcqChain.explanation: Alternation flips color each step; after an odd number of steps you return to the start color, creating a match.

wrongExplanations.2: Counts are irrelevant; parity of the cycle matters.

mcqChain.hint: Follow the color flips around the circle n times.

mcqChain.questionText: When scanning, which condition should trigger output?
mcqChain.explanation: We need a pair of neighbors with the same color, so equality of consecutive positions is the trigger.

wrongExplanations.0: Inequality finds alternating pairs, not what we need.

mcqChain.hint: The task is to find two adjacent chairs with the same color.

mcqChain.questionText: How do we correctly handle the neighbor of the last chair (index n) during the scan?
mcqChain.diagram: Indices: 1 2 ... n-1 n -> neighbor of n is 1

options.label: Compare index n with index n-1.
Use j = (i+1) % n to wrap to 0-based, but adjust indices when printing.
Stop at n-1 to avoid out-of-range access.
Treat the last chair as having no neighbor.

mcqChain.explanation: Using modulo n gives 0 for i=n-1 in 0-based terms; adapt printing to 1-based index.

wrongExplanations.2: We must also check the pair (n,1); skipping misses valid answers.
wrongExplanations.3: In a circle, n is adjacent to 1.

mcqChain.hint: Modulo arithmetic is a clean way to wrap around.

mcqChain.questionText: If multiple valid pairs exist, which index should we output?

options.label: Any valid i; the smallest one is a simple choice.
The largest i to maximize the index.
No output unless there is a unique i.

mcqChain.explanation: The problem allows any valid i. Returning the first found keeps the implementation simple.
mcqChain.hint: No tie-breaking rules are specified; pick a consistent strategy like the first found.

mcqChain.questionText: For n=3 and colors RRB, which i is valid to output?
mcqChain.explanation: (1,2)=R,R is valid. (2,3)=R,B not; (3,1)=B,R not.

wrongExplanations.2: (3,1) are B and R, which differ.

mcqChain.hint: Check each of the three pairs in a circle of length 3.

mcqChain.questionText: If colors[1..n-1] alternate strictly, what must be true about colors[n] and colors[1] when n is odd?

options.label: It depends on the counts of R and B.

mcqChain.explanation: An odd number of flips returns to the starting color, forcing the last to equal the first.

wrongExplanations.1: This would imply an even number of flips, contradicting n odd.

mcqChain.hint: Track the color after n−1 flips and then one more step to reach 1 from n.

mcqChain.questionText: Start by importing what you need for fast input.
mcqChain.explanation: We will read all input via sys.stdin for simplicity and speed.
mcqChain.hint: We plan to read tokens via stdin directly.

mcqChain.questionText: Define the program entry function.
mcqChain.explanation: We'll use a standard main() to encapsulate logic and call it under the __name__ guard.

wrongExplanations.1: We don't need parameters; we'll read from stdin.

mcqChain.hint: A no-argument function is common for script entry.

mcqChain.questionText: Read and tokenize the input efficiently.
mcqChain.explanation: Reading all and splitting gives us n and the color string tokens robustly.

wrongExplanations.1: Multiple input() calls can be slower and trickier for empty lines.
wrongExplanations.2: Single readline doesn't split into tokens for both n and the string.

mcqChain.hint: We want both n and the string as separate tokens easily.

mcqChain.questionText: Parse n and the colors string from tokens.
mcqChain.explanation: n is the first token, then the string; stripping is safe for newline handling.

wrongExplanations.1: n must be converted to int.
wrongExplanations.2: The order of tokens is reversed here.

mcqChain.hint: Assume the first token is the integer n, second is the colors string.

mcqChain.questionText: Guard against accidental extra characters in s (e.g., trailing spaces) by truncating to n if necessary.
mcqChain.explanation: Truncating to n ensures we only consider the provided n chairs.

wrongExplanations.1: Padding invents colors not in input.
wrongExplanations.2: Stripping removes valid characters from ends.

mcqChain.hint: Be conservative: do not add or delete intended characters beyond trimming length.

mcqChain.questionText: Set up the scan over all positions (including wrap-around). Which loop and neighbor index are correct?

options.label:
for i in range(n):
        j = (i + 1) % n

for i in range(n-1):
        j = i + 1

for i in range(1, n+1):
        j = i + 1

mcqChain.explanation: Iterate all i and wrap the neighbor with modulo n so that i=n-1 pairs with 0.

wrongExplanations.3: Modulo n-1 fails to map n-1 to 0 correctly.

mcqChain.hint: We need to compare i with i+1 for all i, wrapping last to 0.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
for i in range(n):
        j = (i + 1) % n

mcqChain.questionText: Write the condition and output when a matching adjacent pair is found. Remember to print a 1-based index and terminate.
mcqChain.explanation: Equality is required; i is 0-based so print i+1. Return to avoid extra output.

wrongExplanations.2: This prints 0-based index; the problem expects 1-based.
wrongExplanations.3: Printing j+1 points to the second chair; the task asks for i.

mcqChain.hint: Adjust for 1-based indexing in the print.

mcqChain.questionText: If no adjacent equal pair is found during the loop (which shouldn't happen for odd n), what should we print as a fallback?
mcqChain.explanation: -1 is a conventional sentinel indicating no solution found.

wrongExplanations.2: n could be valid but we shouldn't assume it without a match.

mcqChain.hint: Use a clear sentinel value for impossible cases.

mcqChain.questionText: Finally, call main only when the script is executed directly.
mcqChain.explanation: The standard entry-point guard ensures main is called appropriately.

wrongExplanations.1: This would run on import too, which is not ideal.

mcqChain.hint: Use the conventional dunder-name check.

mcqChain.questionText: Trace the program for n=5, s=RBRBR. Which index is printed?
mcqChain.explanation: Only (5,1) matches (R,R), so the loop prints 5 and returns.
mcqChain.hint: Check equality for each pair until a match.

mcqChain.questionText: Trace the program for n=7, s=BRRBRBB. Which index is printed?
mcqChain.explanation: (2,3)=R,R is the first match; the program prints 2.
mcqChain.hint: The program stops at the first match in increasing i.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time complexity of the implemented solution with respect to n?
mcqChain.explanation: We perform a single pass over n indices with constant work per index.

wrongExplanations.0: It grows with n due to the scan.

mcqChain.hint: Count how many comparisons are made as n increases.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the additional space complexity (beyond storing the input string)?
mcqChain.explanation: We only use a few variables (indices and temporary).

wrongExplanations.2: We don't allocate structures proportional to n besides the input.

mcqChain.hint: Think about variables used at any time.

mcqChain.questionText: Which edge cases are correctly handled by the code?

options.label: All chairs same color (e.g., RRR...): prints 1.
Strict alternation for first n-1 with n odd: prints n.
Input with extra whitespace/newline after the string.

mcqChain.explanation: The scan detects first equal pair (1 for all-same, n for wrap pair), and input parsing is robust.

wrongExplanations.0: True, but not the only handled case.

mcqChain.hint: Consider parsing and both simple and parity-driven arrangements.

finalCodeSnippets:
for i in range(n):
        j = (i + 1) % n

reasoningChain.question: Why must there be at least one pair of adjacent chairs with the same color when n is odd?
reasoningChain.insight: Strict alternation cannot wrap around an odd cycle; after n flips you return to the starting color, forcing a match.

reasoningChain.question: What simple strategy can always find such a pair given the full string?
reasoningChain.insight: Scan all adjacent pairs (with wrap-around) and stop at the first equality.

reasoningChain.question: How do we handle the circular nature when checking neighbors?
reasoningChain.insight: Use j = (i+1) % n to wrap the neighbor of the last index back to the first.

reasoningChain.question: What index should we print upon finding a match?
reasoningChain.insight: Print the 1-based index i, so convert from 0-based i to i+1.

reasoningChain.question: How do we ensure robust input handling and clear termination?
reasoningChain.insight: Read tokens via sys.stdin, normalize the string length to n, print and return immediately upon finding a match, otherwise print -1.
